response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend a few times monthly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: As a woman who has lived most of her life in Naples and raised a family with strong Christian values, I find it difficult to trust what I see on social media. The world feels like it’s moving too fast, and I often feel anxious when I come across stories that seem too good to be true—like miraculous healings or sudden wealth. I don’t have a college background, but I’ve learned from my parents and church teachings that truth comes from people, not screens. When I see something that looks like a miracle, I pause. I ask myself: Is this something I’ve seen before? Does it make sense in the real world? I don’t like to believe in things that scare me or make me feel sad. My anxiety makes me cautious, so I only check social media a few times a month, usually after my gardening or volunteering work. I prefer real connections—my neighbors, the church, the birds I watch in the garden. I’ve learned that faith and common sense go hand in hand. I don’t share content that feels false, because I know it can hurt others. I believe in honesty, and I try to protect my heart from what doesn’t belong in the world of truth and peace.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is anxious, values faith and family, has limited formal education, and is cautious by nature. Social media is used infrequently, so the focus is on emotional and spiritual well-being. The response reflects her background, emphasizing personal experience, religious grounding, and emotional safety over digital engagement. The tone is reflective, humble, and grounded in real-life values.
response_parsed.tone: reflective, cautious, grounded in faith and personal experience
citations: personal experience of anxiety and media exposure
